title: Cat Circular Hair Loss Patches on Head: Causes and Vet Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn about circular hair loss on your cat's head, including ringworm and allergies. Find out why this is low urgency and how a photo helps triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Circular hair loss in cats is usually caused by conditions like ringworm or allergies and is rarely an emergency unless accompanied by severe inflammation or loss of appetite.

What are the common causes of circular hair loss patches on my cat?

  • Ringworm: A highly contagious fungal infection.
  • Localized allergic reactions.
  • Mite infestations (mange).
  • Feline acne: Common if patches are located near the chin and mouth.
  • Skin appearance: Understanding the look of the skin underneath is key to identifying the cause.

Is circular hair loss in cats considered a medical emergency?

  • The urgency level is typically Low and usually not a life-threatening crisis.
  • Schedule a routine appointment with your veterinarian within a few days.
  • Seek care sooner if the area becomes severely inflamed, starts bleeding, or if your cat stops eating.

How does providing a photo help my veterinarian triage my cat's hair loss?

  • Allows the professional to observe the margins of the hair loss.
  • Identifies the presence of crusting or scaling.
  • Shows the color and condition of the skin.
  • Helps narrow down diagnostic possibilities and determine how quickly your pet needs to be seen.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Focal alopecia on the head and neck in cats may be caused by dermatophytosis, demodicosis (especially *Demodex gatoi*), or flea allergy dermatitis. Diagnosis of demodicosis is made by superficial and deep skin scrapings, although mite numbers may be small. Dermatophyte cultures are essential, as dermatophytosis and demodicosis can occur concurrently. Medical evaluation is indicated in cats with generalized demodicosis to rule out underlying immunosuppressive or metabolic diseases such as feline leukemia virus, feline immunodeficiency virus, diabetes mellitus, or neoplasia. For flea allergy dermatitis in cats without obvious flea infestation, a CBC with differential is recommended, as eosinophilia may be present.

Chapter: Dermatology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 842)

faqs.question: Can ringworm spread to humans?
faqs.answer: Yes, ringworm is a zoonotic fungal infection, meaning it can be transmitted from your cat to you and other household members. Practice good hand hygiene after touching the affected areas.

faqs.question: Should I apply over-the-counter creams before the vet visit?
faqs.answer: It is generally recommended to avoid applying any creams or ointments until a diagnosis is made, as some human medications can be toxic to cats if ingested during grooming.

faqs.question: How do vets test for the cause of hair loss?
faqs.answer: Veterinarians often use a Wood's lamp (UV light) to check for fluorescence, perform skin scrapings, or take a fungal culture to accurately identify the cause.
